title: STANDARD FOR APPLES CXS 299-2010 A d opted in 2010.

sections.section: STANDARD FOR APPLES CXS 299-2010 A d opted in 2010.
sections.summary: Document title: 'Standard for Apples CXS 299-2010', adopted in 2010 (heading/title, not substantive requirements).

Defines the scope: applies to fruits of commercial cultivars of apples (Malus domestica) supplied fresh to consumers after preparation and packaging, and explicitly excludes apples intended for industrial processing.

Sets quality provisions including minimum requirements (e.g., whole, sound, firm, clean, practically free of pests and dehydration, free of abnormal moisture, foreign odours/tastes and temperature damage), maturity criteria to permit continued ripening, and a three-class classification scheme (Extra, Class I, Class II) with allowed defects per class and suggested colour coding percentages.

Lists minimum quality requirements for apples: must be whole (stem may be absent if clean break), sound, firm, clean, practically free of pests and foreign matter, free from abnormal external moisture, foreign odours/tastes, temperature damage and significant dehydration, have colour characteristic of the variety/area, and be suitable to withstand transport and arrive in satisfactory condition.

Requires apples to be at a stage of development that allows continued ripening to the varietal level and specifies that maturity may be verified using parameters such as morphological aspects, firmness and refractometric index.

Classifies apples into three classes ('Extra', I and II) according to defect allowances in the Annex, with 'Extra' for superior, virtually defect‑free fruit, Class I for good quality allowing slight defects, and Class II for fruit meeting only the minimum requirements but retaining essential characteristics.

Defines the 'Extra' Class as apples of superior quality with sound flesh, characteristic of the variety and free of defects except very slight superficial ones that do not affect appearance, quality, keeping quality or presentation.

Requires Class I apples to be of good quality with sound flesh and varietal character while allowing slight defects of shape, colouring or skin provided these do not affect appearance, quality, keeping quality or presentation; notes firmness indicates appropriate maturity and skin/other defects must not exceed Annex limits.

Defines Class II as apples not qualifying for higher classes but meeting the minimum requirements of Section 2.1, permitting defects in shape, colouring and skin so long as the apples retain their essential characteristics for quality, keeping quality and presentation.
Specifies colour codes to be applied in all classes except green and yellow varieties (A: 75% or more, B: 50% or more, C: 25% or more, D: less than 25%) where no national legislation exists.

States size is determined by maximum equatorial diameter or weight with a minimum of 60 mm or 90 g for all varieties and classes, while smaller fruit (down to 50 mm or 70 g) may be accepted only if refractometric index is ≥10.5°Brix.

Allows quality tolerances by class for lots not meeting the indicated class: 'Extra' up to 5% (max 1% decay/internal breakdown), Class I up to 10% (max 1% decay/internal breakdown), and Class II up to 10% failing class/minimum requirements (max 2% decay/internal breakdown); additionally Class II may include up to 2% of fruit with specified minor defects such as cork‑like blemishing, slight unhealed skin damage/cracks or internal pest damage.
Allows a 10% size tolerance by number or weight for all classes under uniformity rules, but this tolerance cannot include fruit below 50 mm or 70 g if the refractometric index is below 10.5°Brix.

Requires package contents to be uniform in origin, quality, size and variety (with 'Extra' also requiring colour uniformity) and sets measurement options for uniformity by diameter or weight, while also requiring protective, new and clean packaging materials (non‑toxic inks/adhesives) and containers that meet quality, hygiene, ventilation and resistance standards in accordance with the Code of Practice CXC 44‑1995.

Mandates that each package contain apples uniform in origin, quality, size and variety (sales packages ≤5 kg may mix varieties/sizes if uniform), that the visible contents be representative, and that uniformity be measured by either diameter or weight according to specified limits.
Sets diameter‑based uniformity limits: maximum diameter difference of 5 mm when the smallest apples are less than 80 mm, and 12 mm when the smallest apples are 80 mm or more.
Non‑substantive transitional item indicating an alternative measurement option ('Or') for assessing uniformity.
Heading introducing the alternative uniformity measurement method by weight (no substantive limits in this brief heading).
Specifies weight‑based uniformity limits between apples in the same package: 15 g if smallest is 90 g, 20 g if 90–<135 g, 30 g if 135–<200 g, 40 g if 200–<300 g, and 50 g if over 300 g.

sections.section: There is no size uniformity for apples packed loose in the package or sales package.
sections.summary: States there is no size uniformity requirement for apples packed loose in the package or in sales packages.

Requires apples to be packed to protect the produce, using new, clean materials that do not cause damage and allowing printed materials only if inks/adhesives are non‑toxic, and mandates packing in compliance with the Code of Practice for Packaging and Transport (CXC 44‑1995).

Requires containers to meet quality, hygiene, ventilation and resistance characteristics to ensure proper handling, shipping and preservation of apples, and that packages be free of foreign matter and smell.
Section heading for marking or labelling with no substantive provisions provided here.

States that consumer packages must comply with the General Standard for the Labelling of Prepackaged Foods (CXS 1‑1985) and that additional specific provisions apply (no specific provisions are detailed in this excerpt).

When produce is not visible from the outside, each package must be labelled with the name of the produce and may additionally show variety, class, colour code (if used) and size/weight or the number of pieces presented in rows and layers.

Annex containing a table of maximum allowances for defects in apples by class ("Extra", Class I, Class II) with percentage and size thresholds for russetting, blemishes/bruising, stem/calyx cracks and elongated defects, plus a definition of russetting and a note that allowances apply only where russetting is not a varietal characteristic.

Table of maximum defect allowances for apples specifying permitted russetting percentages (different types and accumulated limits) and numerical size limits for blemishes/bruises, cured stem/calyx cracks and maximum lengths for elongated defects by class, and noting that russetting is defined and allowances do not apply when russetting is characteristic of the variety.
